chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing dysentery in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), focusing on antibiotics, rehydration, and escalation protocols for resistant or severe cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the first-line and second-line antibiotic regimens for dysentery in SAM.
Learn appropriate rehydration strategies tailored for malnourished children.
Recognize signs of treatment failure and criteria for escalation or referral.
Identify alternative diagnoses in cases of non-response to initial therapy.
key_facts: Empiric antibiotics: Oral ciprofloxacin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 3 days; switch to parenteral ceftriaxone (100 mg/kg/day) if oral therapy fails or is contraindicated.
Rehydration: Use malnutrition-specific solutions like ReSoMal or diluted low-osmolar ORS with potassium/glucose; reassess every 30-60 minutes.
Continue breastfeeding and stabilization feeds (e.g., F-75) during treatment.
Escalation: Reassess at 48 hours if no improvement; consider resistant shigellosis, alternative diagnoses, or systemic complications.
Monitor for worsening edema, increased respiratory rate, or pulse rise during rehydration therapy.
Stool culture and local resistance patterns guide further management in resistant cases.
reference_quote: Treat any visible blood in stool as dysentery until proven otherwise. Severe malnutrition increases risk of invasive bacterial disease and complications, and dehydration assessment can be unreliable; therefore management must combine prompt antibiotics with cautious, closely monitored rehydration and continued feeding.
document_key_topics: Criteria for referral and alternative diagnoses
